coredata.dc:title: Tannin-based thin-film composite membranes integrated with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for butanol dehydration through pervaporation
coredata.dc:creator: Lecaros, Rumwald Leo G.
Valbuena, Reincess E.
Tayo, Lemmuel L.
Hung, Wei-Song
Hu, Chien-Chieh
Tsai, Hui-An
Huang, Shu-Hsien
Lee, Kueir-Rarn
Lai, Juin-Yih
coredata.dc:description: A thin-film composite membrane was prepared through the interfacial polymerization of tannic acid (TA), a natural oligomer, and acyl chloride monomers – terephthaloyl chloride (TPC) or trimesoyl chloride (TMC). Thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes were also prepared by loading a new class of carbon nanoparticles – nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs). The composite membranes were characterized with ATR-FTIR, XPS, SEM and water contact angle. The TA-TMC membrane provided lower flux and better water permselectivity than those prepared with TA-TPC due to availability of more reactive acyl chloride. The concentrations of each reactant used, appropriate loading amount of NGQD and contact time between TA and acyl chloride monomers were all determined and investigated to separate 90/10 wt% n-butanol/water mixture at 25 °C. The TA-TMC was found to have higher surface hydrophilicity due to more hydroxyl groups present. The incorporation of 50 ppm NGQD during the interfacial polymerization enhanced the pervaporation performance by providing additional hydrophilicity and mass transfer resistance to larger permeating molecule. The TA0.075-TMC0.4-NGQD(50) membrane at 3-min contact time had a total flux of 1100 g m−2 h−1 and a water concentration in permeate of 97.1 wt% compared to TA0.075-TPC0.4-NGQD(50) membrane with 2196 g m−2 h−1 and 93.2 wt% in separating 90/10 wt% n-butanol/water mixture at 25 °C. The water permselectivity was also found to be more efficient for isobutanol/water mixtures due to the sterically hindered structure of the alcohol. Furthermore, the TA0.075-TMC0.4-NGQD(50) membrane was also tested to separate all butanol isomer/water mixtures at 25 °C showing better separation performance for sterically hindered or branched butanol isomer. The membrane fabricated herein shows the potential use of cheap and natural oligomer (TA) to produce thin-film composite membranes that provided enough barrier to separate butanol from water through pervaporation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Thin-film composite membrane
Tannic acid
NGQD
Biofuel
Pervaporation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Introduction
Experimental section
Materials
Synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs)
Preparation of aqueous and organic phase solutions
Fabrication of thin-film composite (TFC) and thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes
Characterization of NGQDs and composite membranes
Separation of alcohol/water mixtures by pervaporation
Results and discussion
Characterization of synthesized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs)
Characterization of thin-film and thin-film nanocomposite membranes
Butanol dehydration performance of tannin-based thin-film composite membranes
Effect of varying concentration of tannic acid and acyl chloride monomer
Effect of varying concentration of NGQD
Effect of varying organic phase contact time
Effect of pervaporation operating condition for TA0.075-TMC0.4-NGQD(50) membrane
Effect of feed concentration
Effect of feed temperature
Pervaporation of different butanol isomer/water mixtures
Conclusion
CRediT authorship contribution statement
Acknowledgment
Supplementary data
References

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors wish to sincerely thank the Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan ( MOST 108-2218-E-033-007-MY3 and MOST 109-2811-E-011-513 ) for financially supporting this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of NGQDs: (a) C1s XPS spectrum, (b) N1s XPS spectrum, (c) TEM images of the synthesized NGQDs and (d) particle size distribution.
ATR-FTIR spectra of PES support, Tannic acid, TFC, and TFN membranes.

FESEM surface images of TFC membranes: (a) TA0.075-TPC0.4 and (d) TA0.075-TMC0.4 and TFN membranes: (b) TA0.075-TPC0.4-NGQD(50), (c) TA0.075-TPC0.4-NGQD(100), (e) TA0.075-TMC0.4-NGQD(50) and (f) TA0.075-TMC0.4-NGQD(100).
Water contact angle of PES, TFC and TFN membranes.

Effect of varying concentrations of (a) tannic acid and (b) terephthaloyol chloride (TPC) and (c) comparison between TPC and trimesoyl chloride (TMC) on the pervaporation dehydration of 90/10 wt% n-butanol/water mixture at 25 °C.
Effect of varying NGQD concentration in (a) TA0.075-TPC0.4 and (b) TA0.075-TMC0.4 TFN membranes on the pervaporation dehydration of 90/10 wt% n-butanol/water mixture at 25 °C.

Effect of varying organic phase contact time in (a) TA0.075-TPC0.4-NGQD(50) and (b) TA0.075-TMC0.4-NGQD(50) TFN membranes on the pervaporation dehydration of 90/10 wt% n-butanol/water mixture at 25 °C.
Effect of feed water concentration on the pervaporation performance of TA0.075-TMC0.4-NGQD(50) membrane at 25 °C (a) n-butanol/water and (b) i-butanol/water mixtures.
Effect of feed temperature on the pervaporation performance of TA0.075-TMC0.4-NGQD(50) membrane for 90/10 wt% (a–c) n-butanol/water and (d–f) i-butanol/water mixtures.
Pervaporation separation performance of TA0.075-TMC0.4-NGQD(50) membrane in separating different butanol isomer/water mixtures at 25 °C.
Surface elemental composition of the thin-film composite membranes with different loading of NGQD.
Molar volume and average dynamic cross-section values of butanol isomers.
Comparison of pervaporation performance for the dehydration of n-butanol/water mixtures.

head.ce:title: Tannin-based thin-film composite membranes integrated with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for butanol dehydration through pervaporation

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: R.L.G. Lecaros and R.E. Valbuena are co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A thin-film composite membrane was prepared through the interfacial polymerization of tannic acid (TA), a natural oligomer, and acyl chloride monomers – terephthaloyl chloride (TPC) or trimesoyl chloride (TMC). Thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes were also prepared by loading a new class of carbon nanoparticles – nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs). The composite membranes were characterized with ATR-FTIR, XPS, SEM and water contact angle. The TA-TMC membrane provided lower flux and better water permselectivity than those prepared with TA-TPC due to availability of more reactive acyl chloride. The concentrations of each reactant used, appropriate loading amount of NGQD and contact time between TA and acyl chloride monomers were all determined and investigated to separate 90/10 wt% n-butanol/water mixture at 25 °C. The TA-TMC was found to have higher surface hydrophilicity due to more hydroxyl groups present. The incorporation of 50 ppm NGQD during the interfacial polymerization enhanced the pervaporation performance by providing additional hydrophilicity and mass transfer resistance to larger permeating molecule. The TA0.075-TMC0.4-NGQD(50) membrane at 3-min contact time had a total flux of 1100 g m−2 h−1 and a water concentration in permeate of 97.1 wt% compared to TA0.075-TPC0.4-NGQD(50) membrane with 2196 g m−2 h−1 and 93.2 wt% in separating 90/10 wt% n-butanol/water mixture at 25 °C. The water permselectivity was also found to be more efficient for isobutanol/water mixtures due to the sterically hindered structure of the alcohol. Furthermore, the TA0.075-TMC0.4-NGQD(50) membrane was also tested to separate all butanol isomer/water mixtures at 25 °C showing better separation performance for sterically hindered or branched butanol isomer. The membrane fabricated herein shows the potential use of cheap and natural oligomer (TA) to produce thin-film composite membranes that provided enough barrier to separate butanol from water through pervaporation.

The addition of nitrogen-doped quantum dots (NGQDs) contributed to additional resistance for butanol molecules to permeate through the tannic acid based thin-film.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Fossil fuel is still the world's largest energy source and one of the global concern is its limited availability, cost, and impact on climate change [1,2]. One strategy to lower the consumption of fossil fuels is to use alternative source of fuel from biomass or feedstock producing biofuel. Bioethanol is the most widely used biofuel that can be blended with gasoline at different concentrations. However, biobutanol was found to have better characteristics as its energy value is similar to gasoline and higher than ethanol [2–4]. In addition, due to its non-hygroscopic property it could be blended with gasoline at higher concentrations or even use as pure. Biobutanol could be produced from the conversion of feedstock to fuel by using the bacterium Clostridium sp. strains [5]. For example, in acetone-butanol-ethanol (ABE) fermentation process, the Clostridium acetobutylicum converts sugar into acetone, ethanol and butanol [3,5]. Among the four butanol isomers, the biobutanol are mostly n-butanol and isobutanol (i-butanol) [3]. However, there are also limitations to butanol purification such as: low production yield, cost-intensive recovery and the azeotropic tendency of butanol with water hence making the overall production still not economically feasible to replace fossil fuels.

A way to resolve the purification of biobutanol is through membrane separation technology. An environmentally and efficient way to dehydrate and recover solvents is pervaporation (PV). PV is energy-efficient as can selectively separate components of liquid mixtures even they have close-boiling points or at azeotrope point. The hydrophilic membranes for PV are usually made from synthetic polymers [6–9] and natural polymers [10–12] that are commonly modified with various methods to prevent excessive swelling and enhance water permselectivity. One of the commonly used method to improve the stability and selectivity of the membrane is cross-linking. However, the cross-linked membrane often results to a decline in flux but in return enhances its selectivity. Thus, one way to overcome the low flux in alcohol dehydration, composite membranes that has a thin selective layer are fabricated through interfacial polymerization.

During the interfacial polymerization, the reactants (e.g. monomer) are dissolved in both aqueous and oil (organic) phase. Once in contact they start to form a thin-film at the interface of water and oil. The thin-film could be controlled to produce enough barrier to separate the desired components. The commonly used reactants for interfacial polymerization are diamine and acyl chloride to create polyamide which has shown separation efficiency in various separation process such as nanofiltration [13–15] and pervaporation [16–18]. As an alternative to the synthetic compounds used in interfacial polymerization, natural compounds like tannic acid (TA) can be used. TA is a natural oligomer from plants that possesses numerous phenol groups that are reactive for cross-linking with acyl chloride. It had been shown that the thin-film selective layer from the reaction of TA and terephthaloyl chloride (TPC) on a porous PAN support was effective for organic solvent nanofiltration and offers high flux [19]. TA is also regarded as an environmental pollutant that efforts are made to remove it from water [20,21]. The recovered TA could be utilized for other applications like surface coating and membrane processing [22,23]. Taking the advantage of TA being hydrophilic and cheap, it was chosen to be used as the aqueous phase reactant to cross-link with acyl chlorides like TPC and trimesoyl chloride (TMC) in this study for the dehydration of butanol through pervaporation.

A new class of carbon-based nanoparticles, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs), was also used to further enhance the stability and water permselectivity of the thin-film composite (TFC) membrane. NGQDs possess the similar structure and rich oxygen-containing functional groups of graphene oxide but with nitrogen atoms or nitrogen-containing functional groups. The addition of NGQDs may serve as another barrier to block the passage of larger organic molecules (e.g. alcohol) and allows more water to pass the membrane. It was also previously shown in a polyamide reverse osmosis membrane that the addition of NGQD enhanced the flux and rejection of the membrane [24]. Also in pervaporation, graphene quantum dots were also found to improve the water permselectivity of the membrane [25–28]. In which we also previously show that it affects the free volume characteristics of the polymer matrix [25,26]. Thus, the optimum conditions for the interfacial polymerization of TA with TPC or TMC and loading amounts of NGQD will be investigated for the dehydration of butanol/water mixtures through pervaporation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental section
Materials
ce:section.ce:para: Tannic acid (TA) was obtained from Alfa Aesar (Great Britain, UK). Terephthaloyl chloride (TPC) and trimesoyl chloride (TMC) were purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. (Tokyo, Japan). Citric acid anhydrous (CA) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) pellets were purchased from Showa chemical industry Co. Ltd (Japan). Ammonia in water (25%) was bought from Sigma Aldrich Co. Ltd., (St. Louis, MO, USA). Phosphate buffer solution (PBS) and tert-butanol were obtained from Tedia Company, Inc. (Fairfield, OH, USA). Isobutanol (i-butanol) was purchased from J.T. Baker (Avantor Performance Materials, PA, USA) while n-butanol and n-hexane were acquired from Echo Chemical Co., Ltd. (Taiwan). sec-Butanol was obtained from Acros Organics (Belgium). The polyethersulfone (PES) support membrane was provided by R&D Center for Membrane Technology (Taiwan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The NGQDs were synthesized as described in our previous study and with slight modifications [25,26]. Briefly, 2 g of CA with 0.3 mL of ammonia in water was placed in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The mixture in the autoclave was heated at 210 °C for 3 h in an oven. The liquefied orange solution was naturally cooled at room temperature. Then the solution was neutralized to pH 7.0 with 100 mL of 10 mg mL−1 NaOH aqueous solution. The neutralized solution was dialyzed using CelluSep H1 dialysis tube (MWCO 2000 Da, Membrane Filtration Products, Inc., Senguin, Texas, USA). The NGQDs were kept in refrigerator at 4 °C until use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of aqueous and organic phase solutions
ce:section.ce:para: The aqueous phase solution was prepared by dispersing NGQD at varying concentrations (0, 25, 50, 75, and 100 ppm) in 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (pH 7) by sonication for 15 min. It was then followed by dissolving tannic acid (TA) at varying concentrations (0, 0.05, 0.075, and 0.1 wt%) into the NGQD mixture at room temperature for 30 min under vigorous stirring. The organic phase solution was prepared by separately dissolving terephthaloyl chloride (TPC) and trimesoyl chloride (TMC) at varying concentrations (0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, and 0.6) wt % in a 250 mL bottle containing n-hexane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of thin-film composite (TFC) and thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The PES membrane was soaked in the aqueous phase solution at 30 °C for about 10 min. After removal from the bath, excess solution was drained off using a rubber roller and gently blotting with filter paper. The TA or TA-NGQD treated membranes were then contacted with organic phase solution (TMC or TPC) in n-hexane in varying contact times of 1, 3, 5, 8, and 10 min. Afterwards, the organic phase solution was drained off. The resulting membranes were subjected to thermal annealing at 70 °C for 10 min and followed by vacuum-drying for 3 h to remove excess moisture. The membranes were named as TAx-TPCy-NGQD(Z) or TAx-TPCy-NGQD(Z), where x denotes the concentration of TA (wt%), y for the concentration of acyl chloride monomer (wt%) and z for the concentration of NGQD loaded in ppm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of NGQDs and composite membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The changes in chemical and physical properties of NGQDs were determined through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific, K-Alpha) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai™ G2 F-20 S-TWIN) to characterize the surface chemical composition and particle size, respectively. The particle size distribution using the TEM images was analyzed using ImageJ software. The membranes were characterized through attenuated total reflectance - Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR) (Spectrum One, PerkinElmer), XPS, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Model S-4800 SEM, Hitachi). The water contact angle (WCA) (Model PV-DP, Face Kyowa Interface Science) was measured to characterize the surface hydrophilicity of both TFC and TFN membranes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation of alcohol/water mixtures by pervaporation
ce:section.ce:para:
A continuous pervaporation was used to test the flat-sheet membrane at 25 °C unless otherwise specified. The temperature of the system was controlled through a water bath with a thermostat. An alcohol/water mixture of 90/10 wt % was used as the feed solution and the effective membrane area used was 5 cm2. The permeate side was kept under vacuum using a vacuum pump at 300 Pa. The permeating molecules diffusing through the membrane was collected in a small trap immersed in liquid nitrogen to condense the permeate samples. The total permeation flux (J, g m−2 h−1) was calculated as:
(1)
J =
W
A × t
where W (g) is the weight of the collected permeate at sampling time interval t (h). The composition of both feed and permeate were determined through gas chromatography (China Chromatography, Personal GC-1000).

The relationship of feed operating temperature and individual permeation flux of each component is determined through the Arrhenius equation, as shown in equation (2):
(2)
J i
= A
e
−
E a
R T
where A (
k g
m 2
h
) is the pre-exponential factor, Ea (
k J
m o l
) is the apparent activation energy of component i; R (
k J
m o l
K
) which is equivalent to
8.314 ×
10
− 3
k J
m o l
K
, is the ideal gas constant and T (K) is the operating feed temperature. The apparent activation energy (Ea) was obtained by plotting
ln
(
J i
)
v s
1000 T
.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Characterization of synthesized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) were successfully synthesized through the partial pyrolysis of citric acid in the presence of ammonia. We have previously shown the synthesis of NGQDs and it was proven to possess nitrogen-containing functional groups [25]. Similarly, the partially carbonized citric acid with ammonia has shown functional groups with OGQDS but with nitrogen atoms. This was proven by the presence of C–N bond at 286.1 eV through the C1s XPS spectrum of NGQDs in Fig. 1 a. While the N1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1b) showed the presence of C–N–C at 399.2 eV and graphitic N or N-(C)3 at 400.5 eV proving the successful doping of nitrogen atoms onto the graphitic regions and edges of NGQDs. Furthermore, the FTIR spectrum of NGQDs (Figure S1) showed nitrogen-containing functional groups at 3370 cm−1 (N–H), 1695 cm−1 (Amide CO), and 1400 cm−1 (Amide C–N).

Another indication of the successful synthesis of NGQDs is the formation of the dot-like structure with a particle size of less than 20 nm. The particle size ranges from ~2 to 4 nm with the observable carbon sheets with interlayer spaces (Fig. 1c and d) which is a distinctive characteristic for a graphene and its derivative (e.g. GO).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of thin-film and thin-film nanocomposite membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The interfacial polymerization of tannic acid (TA) and the acyl chloride monomers, terephthaloyl chloride (TPC) and trimesoyl chloride (TMC), on the PES support membrane was analyzed through ATR-FTIR spectroscopy as shown in Fig. 2 . The PES support has a broad peak observed at around 3400 cm−1 due to the addition of polyvinylpyrolidone (PVP) to enhance the formation of pores on the substrate. The presence of the CO peak at 1729 cm−1 and broader –OH stretch at ~3400 cm−1 was observed for the composite membranes with the polyester skin layer formed from the interfacial polymerization of TA and acyl chloride monomers. This indicated the successful reaction between TA and the acyl chloride monomers onto the surface of the PES support membrane.

The TA-TMC membranes were found to have higher peak intensity than with TA-TPC membranes due to the more available oxygen-containing functional groups as the unreacted acyl chloride will be hydrolyzed into carboxylic acid groups, being TMC is a tri-functional acyl chloride. Interestingly, the addition of 50 ppm NGQD in the aqueous phase during the interfacial polymerization reaction formed a polyester skin layer with a broader peak at around 3300 cm−1 which is attributed to the N–H stretching. This proved that the addition of NGQD had played a role during the reaction between TA and the acyl chloride monomers. Additionally, there are small peaks found at around 1000-1300 cm−1 which is attributed to the CO group from ester and C–N bond from the NGQD.

The surface elemental composition of the composite membranes and the effect of integrating NGQD into the polymerization of TA and the acyl chloride monomers were further analyzed through XPS (Table 1 ).

The incorporation of 50 ppm NGQD into the TA aqueous solution decreased the elemental carbon and oxygen composition due to the addition of nitrogen functional groups that has also participated in the interfacial polymerization reaction. The optimum membrane composition using 50 ppm NGQD had an increase in both O/C and N/C elemental ratio which mean that the incorporation of the nanofiller enhanced the adsorption on the membrane surface and intensified the rate of reaction between the aqueous phase and organic phase monomers. However, increasing the loading of NGQD to 100 ppm also increased the carbon and nitrogen compositions and slightly decreased in O/C and O/N elemental ratio. This could be due to the facilitated diffusion of the NGQD into the organic phase thus making the reaction between the filler and reactants at a faster rate. The incorporation of NGQD acted as nanofillers and with an increase in loading amount, the diffusion rate of the monomers increased thus increasing the polymerization reaction rate creating the thin-film skin layer.

The surface morphology of the TFC and TFN membranes prepared with either TPC or TMC were observed through the SEM surface images shown in Fig. 3 . A ridge-and-valley nodules were observed on the surface of the selective layer from membranes that were prepared with either TPC or TMC.

The formed polyester thin-film skin layer resulted with observable pores that could have a negative impact for the dehydration of butanol. However, the TFN membranes loaded with 50 ppm NGQD has a ridge-and-valley structure that appeared to be formed more than in the absence of NGQD (TFC membranes), as confirmed by the AFM images and surface roughness (Rq) in Figure S2. The TA0.075-TPC0.4-NGQD(50) has a measured surface roughness of 3.12 nm while The TA0.075-TMC0.4-NGQD(50) has 3.65 nm. This could be another indication that the NGQD added reacts with the acyl chloride monomers and form a denser structure. Further increasing the loading of NGQD to 100 ppm, the surface became smoother and denser such that the pores were minimized.

The addition of NGQD had been shown to affect both the chemistry (FTIR and XPS) and surface morphology (SEM) of the formed polyester thin-film selective layer. Hence, it is expected also to affect the surface hydrophilicity. The surface hydrophilicity of the TFC and TFN membranes is shown in Fig. 4 .

The PES support membrane has a water contact angle (WCA) of ~60°. Following the interfacial polymerization on the surface of the support membrane, the surface hydrophilicity greatly decreased indicating the creation of a more hydrophilic surface. The polyester thin-film selective layer that was prepared with TMC appeared to have lower WCA measured compared to those prepared with TPC. This is attributed to the reactive tri-acyl chloride groups of TMC while TPC has only two. The unreacted acyl chloride will be converted into carboxylic acid groups that will contribute to the hydrophilicity of the membrane. Hence the conversion of unreacted acyl chloride with TMC is more than TPC.

The TFN membranes, loaded with NGQD, showed an interesting trend where it decreased gradually compared to the TFC membrane (unloaded with NGQD). The lowest WCA measured was with membranes loaded with 50 ppm NGQD. The TA0.075-TPC0.4-NGQD(50) membrane has a WCA of 30.9° while TA0.075-TMC0.4-NGQD(50) membrane is 25°. This indicates that there is an increase on the membrane surface hydrophilicity due to the contribution of additional hydrophilic groups from the nanofiller. This can be aligned with the FTIR results and SEM images where the surface of TFN with 50 ppm NGQD has shown an increase in hydroxyl or amine groups and surface roughness, respectively. However, further increasing the loading after 50 ppm, the contact angle slightly increased up to 33° for TA0.075-TPC0.4-NGQD(100) and 27.8° for TA0.075-TMC0.4-NGQD(100) due to the membrane surface becoming more smooth. Due to the changes on the membrane surface properties, it is expected to have an effect on the pervaporation performance for the removal of water from butanol mixtures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Butanol dehydration performance of tannin-based thin-film composite membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The optimal condition for the interfacial polymerization between TA and the acyl chloride were firstly investigated by varying the concentration of the aqueous phase oligomer (tannic acid), organic monomer (TPC), changing of acyl chloride from TPC to TMC, varying concentration of NGQD, and contact time between aqueous and organic phases. The optimal condition was based on the pervaporation performance of the composite membranes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of varying concentration of tannic acid and acyl chloride monomer
ce:section.ce:para: The pervaporation performance of the thin-film composite (TFC) membranes of varying tannic acid (TA) and terephthaloyl chloride (TPC) for the dehydration of 90/10 wt% n-butanol/water mixture at 25 °C is shown in Fig. 5 a and b. The membrane that was only coated with 0.1 wt% TPC on PES support has a high total flux of 3512 g m−2 h−1 and a low water concentration in permeate of 75.9 wt%. Upon reacting 0.1 wt% TPC with 0.075 wt% TA, the total flux decreased to 2681 g m−2 h−1 and an increase in water concentration in permeate of 90.9 wt% which indicates the formation of a separation layer on the PES support membrane. However, further increasing the TA concentration to 0.1 wt%, the flux increased to 2958 g m−2 h−1 with a dramatic decrease in the water concentration in permeate to 74.5 wt%. The thin-film layer with 0.1 wt% TA may have formed a loose structure due to the increased amount of phenol groups that penetrated the polymerized layer even before it reacted with the acyl chloride monomer. The excessive amount of tannic acid monomer in aqueous solution may have decreased the degree of cross-linking in the interfacial polymerization of the thin film due to the insufficient amount of acyl chloride. This will create regions where no cross-linking occurred and formed pores that caused the increase in flux and decrease in selectivity. Thus the optimum TA concentration used was 0.075 wt%.

The TA concentration was maintained at 0.075 wt% followed by varying the concentration of TPC (Fig. 5b). When the TPC concentration was increased from 0.1 wt% to 0.4 wt%, a higher polymerization rate was achieved which means that the deposited layer was dense and favors the water permselectivity of the membrane. At the TPC concentration of 0.4 wt%, a flux of 2901 g m−2 h−1 and a water concentration in permeate of 92.8 wt% was achieved. This suggests that there is a sufficient amount of TA reacting with TPC. When the TPC concentration was increased to 0.6 wt%, the water permselectivity of the membrane suffered. This could be attributed to the creation of enlarged free volume spaces due to the availability of bulkier groups from the polymerization process.

The TPC has two reactive acyl chloride groups while TMC has three. The acyl chloride monomers are commonly used for the polymerization of polyamides through interfacial interaction. Fig. 5c shows the comparison of pervaporation performance using TPC and TMC as organic phase monomers at the same content. Using TPC resulted to a higher flux but lower water concentration in permeate compared when TMC was used. The flux declined from 2901 g m−2 h−1 to 1204 g m−2 h−1 while the water concentration in permeate increased from 92.8 wt% to 95.3 wt% in dehydrating 90/10 wt% n-butanol/water mixture at 25 °C. The TFC membrane, TA0.075-TMC0.4, appeared to form a denser selective layer. During interfacial polymerization, more acyl chloride participated in the reaction with the excess phenol groups of TA. This resulted to the decrease in total flux and the enhancement of water permselectivity was due to the higher surface hydrophilicity as confirmed by the WCA results (Fig. 4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of varying concentration of NGQD
ce:section.ce:para: The optimum NGQD loading for the thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes in using both TPC and TMC were investigated for the dehydration of 90/10 wt% n-butanol/water mixtures at 25 °C (Fig. 6 ). In using both acyl chloride monomers, the total flux decreased with an increase in NGQD loading. The pervaportion performance was found to be optimum when the NGQD loading was at 50 ppm. The TA0.075-TPC0.4-NGQD(50) membrane has a total flux of 2206 g m−2 h−1 and a water concentration in permeate of 93.3 wt% while the TA0.075-TMC0.4-NGQD(50) has a performance of 1100 g m−2 h−1 and 97.1 wt%. The effect of increasing NGQD decreased the total flux due to the formation of a tortuous pathway for the permeating molecules thus providing additional mass transfer resistance [26]. The creation of a more tortuous pathway could be observed by the continuous drop in total while maintaining the water concentration in permeate. For the TFN membranes prepared with TPC, the water permselectivity was not greatly affected. However, for the TFN prepared with TMC as shown a slight increase in water concentration in permeate where it was increased from 95.3 wt% to 97.1 wt%. Again the TA-TMC membranes appeared to have higher water permselectivity even NGQD was added due to the presence of more hydrophilic functional groups that can attract water molecules. Further increasing the NGQD loading to more than 50 ppm was found to decrease the water permselectivity. This could be attributed to the creation of larger free volume caused by the particles within the polymer chains. Thus, for the optimum NGQD loading was set to 50 ppm for both TFN membrane systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of varying organic phase contact time
ce:section.ce:para: The contact time of organic phase on the surface to react with TA could also affect the formation of the selective layer and pervaporation dehydration performance as shown in Fig. 7 . It can be observed that at 1-min contact time, either with TPC or TMC, achieved the highest total flux but the lowest water concentration in permeate. The low water permselectivity at shorter contact time could be the cause of less reaction between the reactants thus creating a loose structure. While extending the contact time to 3 min, the water concentration in permeate slightly increased with a decrease in total flux. This is an indication of creating a denser selective layer. After 3-min contact time, the water concentration in permeate was maintained (~93 wt% for TA0.075-TPC0.4-NGQD(50) and ~97 wt% for TA0.075-TPC0.4-NGQD(50)) with a continuous decline in total flux hinting the formation of a denser structure. This shows that the 3-min contact time was enough for both reactants to interact and form a dense structure. Again, comparing using TPC with TMC, the membranes prepared with TMC showed lower flux contributing from more acyl chloride groups reacting with TA. Hence, the TFN membrane, TA0.075-TMC0.4-NGQD(50), with a 3-min contact time was used to further evaluate the pervaporation operating condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pervaporation operating condition for TA0.075-TMC0.4-NGQD(50) membrane
Effect of feed concentration
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of water content in feed on the pervaporation performance of TA0.075-TMC0.4-NGQD(50) membrane was performed with both butanol isomers/water mixtures, n-butanol and i-butanol, at 25 °C as shown in Fig. 8 . For both alcohol/water mixtures, the total flux was observed to increase and that the water permselectivity declined with the increase of water in feed from 5 wt% to 20 wt%. This is a common observation for hydrophilic membranes in pervaporation. For n-butanol/water mixtures, the flux increased from 739 g m−2 h−1 to 2572 g m−2 h−1 from 5 wt% to 20 wt% water in feed while the water concentration in permeate declined from 97.2 wt% to 95.6 wt%. On the other hand, the total flux of i-butanol/water mixtures increased from 606 g m−2 h−1 to 2540 g m−2 h−1 with a water concentration in permeate from 99.4 wt% to 96.3 wt%. The addition of NGQD into the polymerization of TA and TMC made the membrane more hydrophilic. Thus water molecules have enhanced interaction with the membrane leading to swelling and enhanced polymer chain mobility. The plasticizing effect also increases the free volume of the membrane thus allowing the transport of both water and alcohol. In addition to the increase in free volume that would allow the transport of both permeating molecules, the increase in flux was also caused by the increase of partial pressure of water at the feed side while the permeate side was kept under vacuum condition. This enhances the driving force for the water molecules thus keeping high water content in permeate. The water concentration in permeate was higher for the i-butanol/water mixture than n-butanol/water mixture due to the sterically hindered structure of i-butanol making it to diffuse slower due to enhanced resistance. Also, i-butanol has weak intermolecular forces and higher vapor pressure which will provide more vapor at 25 °C compared to n-butanol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of feed temperature
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of feed temperature on the pervaporation performance of TA0.075-TMC0.4-NGQD(50) membrane was performed with both butanol isomers/water mixture (90/10 wt%), n-butanol (Fig. 9 a–c) and i-butanol (Fig. 9d–f). A rapid increase in total flux was observed for both butanol isomer/water mixtures when the temperature was raised from 25 °C to 70 °C while the water concentration in permeate declined. The increase in total flux was caused by the enhanced thermal motion of the permeating molecules, increase in partial pressure of each component and increased polymer chain mobility thus promoting faster permeation, enhanced driving force and creating more free volume spaces. Taking all of these reasons could also explain the drop in water permselectivity. As seen in Fig. 9b and d, the flux of the alcohol also increased with temperature. Nevertheless, the membrane still kept a high water permselectivity due to the reasonable water concentration in permeate with high flux for the dehydration of butanol/water mixtures.

Further analyzing the effect of temperature with the effect of temperature on the separation process, the Arrhenius plot with the apparent activation energies calculated from the slope of the line is shown in Fig. 9c and f. The apparent activation energy of n-butanol (40.33 kJ mol−1) was found to be lower than i-butanol (59.20 kJ mol−1). This could be correlated with i-butanol having a sterically hindered structure that will have difficulty passing through the membrane due to greater mass transfer resistance. This also explains the lower permeation flux with i-butanol/water mixtures. Hence, the lower activation energy of water, for both alcohol/water mixtures, suggests the easier diffusion of water molecules with the change in temperature. Regardless, the individual fluxes for alcohol is still lower compared with water showing good water permselectivity of the membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pervaporation of different butanol isomer/water mixtures
ce:section.ce:para: The TFN membrane, TA0.075-TMC0.4-NGQD(50), was tested to separate 90/10 wt% of all four butanol isomer/water mixtures (e.g. n-butanol, i-butanol, sec-butanol and tert-butanol) as shown in Fig. 10 . The total flux was found to decrease with from the linear to branched structures: n-butanol > i-butanol > sec-butanol > tert-butanol. While the water concentration in permeate followed the reverse trend where a higher water permselectivity was observed for the branched structure than linear structure: tert-butanol > i-butanol > sec-butanol > n-butanol. Hence, for isomeric alcohols, the linear alcohol resulted to a higher permeation flux compared to the sterically hindered or branched alcohol. The tert-butanol had the highest water concentration in permeate due to its bulky structure and greater mass transfer resistance leading to lower permeation flux. This trend could be correlated with the chemical structure, molar volume and average dynamic cross-section of the molecule. Where the smaller the molar volume and average dynamic cross-section would lead to faster permeation and lower selectivity. However, it was found that the selectivity only followed the trend of the molar volume and average dynamic cross-section values. The sec-butanol/water mixture resulted to a lower flux than with i-butanol/water mixture could be further attributed to the position of the hydroxyl group on the structure. Like with n-butanol, the hydroxyl group of i-butanol is at the terminal carbon while for sec-butanol is at the second carbon. The hydroxyl group for both n-butanol and i-butanol are more free to interact with the membrane unlike the sterically hindered alcohols. Thus, the branched alcohol (i.e., sec-butanol and tert-butanol) resulted to a lower flux due to additional structural resistance during permeation. The molar volume and average dynamic cross-section of butanol isomers are listed in Table 2 .

The TA0.075-TMC0.4-NGQD(50) membrane has shown good stability under long-term operation for 168 h at 25 °C in separating 90/10 wt% n-butanol/water mixture as shown in Figure S3. The total flux was maintained at ~1100 g m−2 h−1 with a water concentration in permeate maintained at ~97%. The pervaporation dehydration performance of this TFN membrane for the separation of n-butanol/water mixtures was compared to other pervaporation membranes that separated high n-butanol concentration in feed as shown in Table 3 . The TA0.075-TMC0.4-NGQD(50) membrane may have offered low water concentration in permeate but has shown high total flux. Nevertheless, this study offered the possibility of using thin-film composite and thin-film nanocomposite membranes from the reaction of natural compound (i.e., tannic acid) with acyl chloride. In addition, the incorporation of NGQDs in the membrane could provide insights to use as an alternative as inorganic fillers compared to using the widely studied 2-dimensional graphene oxide.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para: A TFN membrane with tannic acid (TA), acyl chloride monomers (TPC or TMC), and a new-class of carbon nanomaterial – nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQD) was fabricated for the dehydration of 90/10 wt% butanol/water mixtures. The reaction of more acyl chloride group with TA, using TMC, provided a denser and more water permselective membrane. The addition of NGQD provided additional interaction with the reactants during the interfacial polymerization process. Hence, affecting both physical and chemical properties of the thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membrane. The TA0.075-TMC0.4-NGQD(50) membrane, loaded with 50 ppm NGQD, showed rough membrane surface, smaller pores and more hydrophilic than the pristine thin-film composite (TFC) membrane. The NGQD improved the affinity of the membrane to water molecules and blocked the transport of larger alcohol molecules through the membrane. The high water permeselectivity of the TFN membrane could also be observed even at increasing feed temperature and water content. Thus, providing a membrane with high flux and selectivity for the separation of the valuable bio-alcohol and water. Moreover, the TFN membrane was also tested for the dehydration of different butanol isomers. The results were correlated with the molar volume and average dynamic cross-section of the permeating molecule. That is, the more linear and less sterically hindered the permeating molecule is the faster is its permeation through the membrane. Overall, it was shown herein the potential use of natural rich with phenolic groups to form a thin skin layer when reacted with acyl chloride monomers creating enough barrier to separate large alcohol such as butanol from water. The membrane fabricated improved with NGQDs provided high flux and water permselectivity for the dehydration of butanol/water mixtures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
ce:section.ce:para: Rumwald Leo G. Lecaros: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Visualization. Reincess E. Valbuena: Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Visualization. Lemmuel L. Tayo: Supervision. Wei-Song Hung: Supervision. Chien-Chieh Hu: Supervision. Hui-An Tsai: Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Shu-Hsien Huang: Supervision. Kueir-Rarn Lee: Conceptualization, Validation, Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Juin-Yih Lai: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to sincerely thank the Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan (MOST 108-2218-E-033-007-MY3 and MOST 109-2811-E-011-513) for financially supporting this work.
